svg.font-family: -apple-system, Segoe UI, Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif
svg.aria-label: The two possible legal theories behind the Fable 5 / Mythos 5 export order and the structural flaw in each
svg.title: Whatever statute the Lutnick directive rests on, the same problem recurs: remote access to a hosted model is hard to fit the chosen authority.
svg.text: The ban's legal footing — two theories, two flaws
The order's text and stated authority are not public. Analysts map it to one of two paths; each has a structural problem.
Theory A · Export Administration Regs (EAR)
§744.22 military-intelligence end-use controls —
treat foreign access to the model as a controlled “export.”
Theory B · IEEPA emergency authority
declare a national-emergency basis to restrict a
text-generating service reaching foreign persons.
Flaw: must be “subject to the EAR” first
Commerce's own three prior Advisory Opinions read
§734.13/.18 to say remote access to hosted software
is not an export. The order would contradict them.
Flaw: informational-materials carve-out
IEEPA's Berman exemption shields informational
output, and the route needs a declared emergency —
which the administration did not invoke.
Same problem either way: a hosted model isn't a shipped “item.”
Contested footing strengthens the case for a scoped, verified-access fix over indefinite darkness.
This is a different lever than the Jun-24 political reframing (NSA/Glasswing): that pushed on the ban's *premise*; this pushes on its *legal authority*.
Sources: Just Security, CSIS, IAPP, Lawfare (Jun 2026). The order itself remains unpublished; mappings are analysts' best reading.
